name: Isamu Noguchi and the Stone Sculpture of Early India
description:
Isamu Noguchi in India, c. 1970s. The Noguchi Museum Archives, 08014. © The Isamu Noguchi Foundation and Garden Museum, New York / Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York.
Isamu Noguchi wrote in 1949 that the site of Mahabalipuram was his “first and most authentic lesson.” Between then and his death in 1988, Noguchi visited India at least eleven times. Yet there has been little investigation into the nature of this long and deep connection, and the impact it had on the sculptor’s artistic philosophy and practice.

In this talk, Kalyani Madhura Ramachandran, art historian and Postdoctoral fellow at NYU Shanghai, sheds light on the sculptor’s enduring interest in the art of early India, including Sanchi, Elephanta, and Mahabalipuram, among numerous other ancient sites, and traces the centrality of the “matter of sculpture” in his approach. In looking at and alongside Noguchi, this talk expands our current understanding of the sculptor’s work and calls for material approaches to the study of sculpture from early India.

This talk will be followed by a discussion with art historian and professor Dipti Khera, and a Q&A with the audience.

This programme at MAP is part of A Closer Look, a monthly series of research-focused talks by artists, curators and art historians focusing on visual cultures of South Asia.

name: Many Ways of Seeing
description:
How do experts from different fields read landscapes differently? What can an ecologist reveal about an artwork that we might otherwise miss?

If these questions intrigue you, join us for a walkthrough of Beneath the Turning Sky followed by an exclusive collection viewing of artworks from MAP’s collection that respond to ecology and are in line with the curatorial vision of the exhibition.

The walkthrough takes the form of a conversation between one of the exhibition’s curators, Priya Chauhan, and ecologist Dr Manan Bhan. While Dr Bhan connects the artworks to his research in ecology, Priya will draw our attention to the artistic details and curatorial choices that allow us to arrive at these ecological readings. Together, they invite us to see how different disciplines can offer distinct yet complementary ways of looking at the same landscape.

The programme concludes with a guided viewing of works from MAP’s collection, led by an in-house expert from the Collections team. Bringing together artworks by artists such as Jyoti Bhatt and Mantu Das, this exclusive viewing offers a chance to discover how ecological questions continue to unfold across the museum’s wider collection.

name: Trees as Climate Witness
description:
Trees are more than sources of shade, beauty and sustenance—they are living indicators of environmental change. Join us for an engaging workshop with SeasonWatch, a pan-India citizen science initiative that studies the seasonal cycles of trees to better understand the impacts of climate change. Through observing the leaves, flowers and fruits of common trees, participants will discover how seasonal changes reveal shifts in our environment and why long-term monitoring is vital for climate research.

The workshop combines both indoor and outdoor learning, offering insight into how everyday observations can contribute to scientific research.



This event is in conjunction with our ongoing permanent exhibition Beneath the Turning Sky.

name: Reading Together: A Disability Pride Reading Circle
description:
What can reading together make possible? This reading circle, facilitated by Mira Brunner, brings participants together to engage with selected excerpts from books, essays, memoirs, and poetry by disabled writers and thinkers. Through shared reading and conversation, the session explores themes of identity, access, care, joy, resistance, and belonging, creating space for reflection and the exchange of perspectives. No prior reading is required, and participants are invited to join with curiosity and openness.
This event is presented as part of MAP's Disability Pride Month 2026 programming.

name: Drawing The Unseen
description:
Inspired by Beneath the Turning Sky, Drawing the Unseen is a workshop led by Anjali Pujari for Deaf adults that invites participants to engage with the exhibition through slow looking, reflection, and creative exploration.

Moving beyond the search for a single meaning, the workshop encourages participants to observe details such as lines, shapes, textures, patterns, and empty spaces, while reflecting on themes of memory, ecology, interconnectedness, and imagination. Through guided prompts, participants will consider what cannot always be seen but can be sensed, remembered, or imagined.

Responding to the exhibition using a range of art materials, each participant will create an individual artwork that reflects their own observations and experiences. The workshop celebrates multiple ways of seeing, making, and interpreting, creating an inclusive space for creativity, conversation, and personal expression.

Designed specifically for Deaf adults, Drawing the Unseen,  offers an opportunity to connect with art at an unhurried pace and discover new ways of relating to the world through observation, imagination, and making.

For ISL translation, please click here.

name: BTS Reading Circle
description:
Image credits: Aparna Nori 
From enchanted lands, curses, and boons to the realities of the modern climate crisis, this reading circle invites participants to reflect on our evolving relationship with the natural worl. We invite you to explore stories and ideas that provoke, challenge, and inspire uncovering ways of understanding environmental change while drawing on collective wisdom, resistance, and imagination to learn how to listen to the planet again.

Drawing from the themes explored in Beneath the Turning Sky the sessions engage with ecology and climate through a range of texts spanning folklore, ecofeminism, Indigenous knowledge systems, environmental history, and climate critique.

Upon registration, participants will be sent text extracts that will be discussed during the session. 

This event is in conjunction with our ongoing permanent exhibition Beneath the Turning Sky.

name: Memory, Myth & Making
description:
Facilitated by The Open Studio Project, a collaborative practice by artists and art psychotherapists Devika Sundar and Roshni Bhatia, this workshop invites participants into an exploration of memory, myth, and imagination through creative practice.

Beginning with a shared exploration of the exhibition Beneath the Turning Sky, participants will draw from their own lived experiences, memories, and imagination to create visual narratives that weave together inner and outer worlds and landscapes. Through image-making, they are invited to explore the meeting points between memory, myth, place, and the natural world, considering how stories—both inherited and imagined—shape the ways we understand ourselves and the environments we inhabit. Rather than focusing on artistic skill or finished artworks, the workshop emphasizes the creative process as a space for curiosity, reflection, dialogue and meaning-making.

Taking place as part of Disability Pride Month, the workshop is grounded in the values of accessibility, inclusivity, and care. Designed as a welcoming space for creativity and conversation, it embraces diverse ways of learning, making, and participating, inviting everyone to engage at their own pace and in ways that feel meaningful to them.

No prior artistic experience is required, and all materials will be provided.

name: Who Is Responsible for Belonging?
description:
How do the ways we classify people shape experiences of belonging? Labels such as normal, disabled, or independent often influence how people are perceived, included, and supported. In this conversation, Bela Joshi and Sneha Joshi draw on their work at Colours Centre for Learning and their lived experiences as a mother and sibling of an autistic person to reflect on disability, care, and inclusion. Together, they examine how social attitudes, inaccessible environments, and everyday systems shape experiences of disability, while considering what it means to build communities where responsibility for inclusion is shared.
Presented as part of MAP's Disability Pride Month 2026 programming.

name: Up, Close and Centre: Nataraja
description:
Ever wondered why most museums have Nataraja or Dancing Shiva in their collections? It is a result of multiple phenomena that happened during the 18th century. Art history, modern dance, physics, West’s fascination for Eastern culture and more, parallely played a part.

This month’s edition of Up, Close and Centre will feature Abiraami from the MAP team. She looks at Nataraja from our second permanent exhibition Beneath the Turning Sky, through the MAP Collection.

Up, Close & Centre is a series that allows audiences to engage with art in deeper and more meaningful ways by delving into the context, narratives and makings of one artwork from our collection, chosen by our team members.

name: Speculative Worlds: A Creative Workshop
description:
Inspired by Beneath the Turning Sky and Luru Magazine's third issue, Nature & Nurture, this creative workshop invites participants to explore the intersections of memory, ecology, and imagination through art-making.

Beginning with an exploration of the exhibition, participants will draw from the artworks, their own lived experiences, and the natural world to create imagined beings, landscapes, and visual narratives. Through speculative image-making, the workshop encourages participants to think beyond familiar ways of seeing and consider new relationships between people, place, and the more-than-human world.

Taking place as part of Disability Pride Month, the workshop is grounded in the values of accessibility, inclusivity, and care. Designed as a welcoming space for creativity and conversation, it embraces diverse ways of learning, making, and participating, inviting everyone to engage at their own pace and in ways that feel meaningful to them.

No prior artistic experience is required, and all materials will be provided.
